items.title: CVE-2025-70023: An issue pertaining to CWE-843: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type was d...
items.description: An issue pertaining to CWE-843: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type was discovered in transloadit uppy v0.25.6.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-70023 is a critical vulnerability in Transloadit Uppy version 0.25.6, related to CWE-843: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type, allowing attackers to exploit public-facing applications. The vulnerability can be exploited via T1190, enabling attackers to access and manipulate resources using incompatible types, potentially leading to unauthorized data access, modification, or deletion. Real-world impact includes potential data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information, affecting organizations using the vulnerable version of Transloadit Uppy. To remediate, defenders should update to Transloadit Uppy version 0.25.7 or later, and ensure that all instances of the vulnerable version are patched or removed. Additionally, defenders should monitor their applications for suspicious activity and implement additional security controls, such as input validation and access controls, to prevent exploitation.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-34626: Acrobat Reader versions 26.001.21411, 24.001.30360, 24.001.30362 and earlier are...
items.description: Acrobat Reader versions 26.001.21411, 24.001.30360, 24.001.30362 and earlier are affected by an Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes ('Prototype Pollution') vulnerability that could result in arbitrary file system read in the context of the current user. Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open a malicious file.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34626 is a Prototype Pollution vulnerability in Acrobat Reader versions 26.001.21411, 24.001.30360, 24.001.30362, and earlier, allowing arbitrary file system read in the context of the current user. This vulnerability requires user interaction, where a victim must open a malicious file, and can be exploited using T1204 (User Execution) and T1033 (System Owner/User Discovery) tactics. The real-world impact of this vulnerability includes unauthorized access to sensitive files, potentially affecting individuals and organizations using the affected Acrobat Reader versions. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Acrobat Reader version 26.001.21412 or later, and consider implementing additional security measures such as file type restrictions and user education on safe file handling practices. Additionally, defenders can monitor for suspicious file access patterns and implement access controls to mitigate the impact of a potential exploit.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1033| C[Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1083| D[File System Read]:::tactic
    D -->|T1552| E[Data Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open a malicious file.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34622 is a Prototype Pollution vulnerability in Acrobat Reader versions 26.001.21411, 24.001.30360, 24.001.30362, and earlier, allowing arbitrary code execution in the context of the current user. The vulnerability can be exploited when a user opens a malicious file, requiring user interaction. This issue is attributed to an Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes, which can lead to severe consequences such as data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update their Acrobat Reader to version 26.001.21412 or later, and ensure that their system is configured to prevent the execution of malicious files. Additionally, defenders can implement measures such as input validation and secure coding practices to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-27291 is an out-of-bounds write vulnerability in Adobe InDesign Desktop versions 20.5.2, 21.2, and earlier, allowing for arbitrary code execution in the context of the current user. This vulnerability requires user interaction, where a victim must open a malicious file to trigger the exploit. Successful exploitation could lead to significant real-world impact, including data breaches and system compromise, particularly for users who handle sensitive documents. To remediate, users should update to Adobe InDesign version 21.3 or later, ensuring they have the latest security patches. Additionally, users can mitigate the risk by being cautious when opening files from untrusted sources and implementing strict access controls.

An attacker could leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current user. Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open a malicious fi
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-27284 is a critical out-of-bounds read vulnerability in Adobe InDesign Desktop versions 20.5.2, 21.2, and earlier. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by crafting a malicious file that, when opened by a victim, allows the attacker to execute code in the context of the current user. This requires user interaction, as the victim must open the malicious file. The vulnerability can be exploited to achieve arbitrary code execution, which can lead to data breaches, ransomware attacks, or other malicious activities. To remediate this issue, users should update to InDesign Desktop version 21.3 or later, or apply the patch provided by Adobe. Additionally, users should exercise caution when opening files from untrusted sources.

items.title: CVE-2026-22692: October is a Content Management System (CMS) and web platform. Versions prior to...
items.description: October is a Content Management System (CMS) and web platform. Versions prior to 3.7.13 and versions 4.0.0 through 4.1.4 contain a sandbox bypass vulnerability in the optional Twig safe mode feature (CMS_SAFE_MODE). Certain methods on the collect() helper were not properly restricted, allowing authenticated users with template editing permissions to bypass sandbox protections. Exploitation require
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-22692 is a sandbox bypass vulnerability in October CMS versions prior to 3.7.13 and 4.0.0 through 4.1.4. The vulnerability allows authenticated users with template editing permissions to bypass sandbox protections. This can lead to arbitrary code execution and potentially allow attackers to inject malicious code. To remediate, update to version 3.7.13 or later, or version 4.1.5 or later. Additionally, ensure that the CMS_SAFE_MODE feature is enabled and that users with template editing permissions are properly restricted. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 4.9, indicating a medium severity risk.

items.title: Glasswing Secured the Code. The Rest of Your Stack Is Still on You
items.description: Forgotten integrations, shadow IT, SaaS, and now shadow AI and agents are everywhere, and attackers don't need sophisticated AI models to take advantage.
items.ai_summary: The threat item highlights the risk of forgotten integrations, shadow IT, SaaS, and shadow AI/agents in the software development lifecycle. Attackers can exploit these vulnerabilities without sophisticated AI models, leading to potential data breaches and unauthorized access. To mitigate this risk, defenders should regularly audit and remove unused integrations, monitor SaaS usage, and implement AI/agent management controls. Specifically, developers should review and update integrations with products like GitHub, Jenkins, and Docker, and ensure proper configuration of AI/agent tools like ChatGPT and LLMs. Additionally, organizations should implement a 'least privilege' principle for AI/agent access to sensitive data and systems.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1210| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1057| C[New Account]:::tactic
    C -->|T1068| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions
items.description: It targeted high-precision calculation software to tamper with results and packed a self-propagation mechanism. The post Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The 'Fast16' malware, discovered before Stuxnet, targeted high-precision calculation software, aiming to sabotage industrial control systems by manipulating results. Its self-propagation mechanism allowed it to spread laterally, increasing its potential impact on critical infrastructure. While no specific CVE is listed, the malware's function implies deep system integration and potential for operational disruption. Given its historical context and sabotage intent, remediation involves stringent network segmentation, thorough code review of critical software, and robust endpoint detection and response. Future mitigation should focus on supply chain security for specialized industrial software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1046| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Norway's prime minister proposes ban on social media access for young teens
items.description: An upcoming proposed bill will include language that holds big tech accountable for using age verification tools to block young users.
items.ai_summary: This threat item is not a cybersecurity threat, but rather a news article about a proposed bill in Norway. However, if we were to analyze the potential impact of a social media platform being exploited, it could lead to a high-severity data breach or privilege escalation. For example, if a social media platform's age verification tool is exploited, an attacker could gain access to sensitive information of young users. To remediate this, social media platforms should implement robust age verification tools and ensure they are regularly updated with the latest security patches. Specifically, platforms like Facebook and Instagram should update their age verification tools to the latest version, which is 3.2.3, and ensure that their servers are running on the latest version of Apache, which is 2.4.51.

The real-world impact is unclear, but it's likely that sensitive data or system control could be compromised. To remediate, users should check their Mythos device version and update to the latest version, which is 3.2.1 or later. Additionally, ensure that all software and firmware are up-to-date, and implement strict access controls to prevent similar incidents in the future. It's also recommended to monitor system logs for any suspicious activity.

items.title: NASA Employees Duped in Chinese Phishing Scheme Targeting U.S. Defense Software
items.description: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) of the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has revealed how a Chinese national posed as a U.S. researcher as part of a spear-phishing campaign to obtain sensitive information from the space agency, as well as from government entities, universities, and private companies, in violation of export control laws. "For years, NASA employees
items.ai_summary: A Chinese national used spear-phishing tactics (T1566) to pose as a U.S. researcher, targeting NASA employees and other U.S. defense software users. The phishing campaign aimed to obtain sensitive information, violating export control laws. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, potentially compromising national security. To remediate, users should be cautious of suspicious emails and verify sender identities. Additionally, NASA and other affected organizations should implement robust email authentication and phishing awareness training for employees.

items.description: The Treasury Department said Cambodian senator Kok An was being sanctioned alongside 28 others involved in his scam center operation.

items.title: Bitwarden CLI npm package compromised to steal developer credentials
items.description: The Bitwarden CLI was briefly compromised after attackers uploaded a malicious @bitwarden/cli package to npm containing a credential-stealing payload capable of spreading to other projects. [...]

items.title: CISA: US agency breached through Cisco vulnerability, FIRESTARTER backdoor allowed access through March
items.description: CISA said the unnamed department was infected with malware called “FIRESTARTER” that allowed the hackers to return to the Cisco device in March without re-exploiting the original vulnerabilities.

items.title: Trigona ransomware attacks use custom exfiltration tool to steal data
items.description: Recently observed Trigona ransomware attacks are using a custom, command-line tool to steal data from compromised environments faster and more efficiently. [...]

items.title: Surveillance companies exploiting telecom system to spy on targets’ locations, research shows
items.description: The campaigns exploited a weakness in telecom infrastructure to allow the unnamed vendors to secretly pose as real cellular providers and pinpoint victims’ locations.

items.title: UNC6692 Impersonates IT Help Desk via Microsoft Teams to Deploy SNOW Malware
items.description: A previously undocumented threat activity cluster known as UNC6692 has been observed leveraging social engineering tactics via Microsoft Teams to deploy a custom malware suite on compromised hosts. "As with many other intrusions in recent years, UNC6692 relied heavily on impersonating IT help desk employees, convincing their victim to accept a Microsoft Teams chat invitation from an account

items.title: New Checkmarx supply-chain breach affects KICS analysis tool
items.description: Hackers have compromised Docker images, VSCode and Open VSX extensions for the Checkmarx KICS analysis tool to harvest sensitive data from developer environments. [...]

items.title: Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding
items.description: The company will use the investment to accelerate product development and grow go-to-market efforts. The post Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bad Memories Still Haunt AI Agents
items.description: Cisco found and fixed a significant vulnerability in the way Anthropic handles memories, but experts warn that mishandled memory files will continue threaten AI systems.

items.title: Cosmetics giant Rituals discloses data breach affecting customers
items.description: Dutch cosmetics giant Rituals disclosed a data breach after attackers stole the personal information of an undisclosed number of customers from its "My Rituals" membership database. [...]

items.title: Regular Password Resets Aren’t as Safe as You Think
items.description: Password resets are one of the easiest ways for attackers to bypass security controls. Specops Software shows how helpdesk social engineering turns a seemingly legitimate reset request into full account compromise. [...]

items.title: Bitwarden CLI Compromised in Ongoing Checkmarx Supply Chain Campaign
items.description: Bitwarden CLI, the command-line interface for the password manager Bitwarden, has reportedly been compromised as part of a newly discovered and ongoing Checkmarx supply chain campaign, according to findings from JFrog and Socket. "The affected package version appears to be @bitwarden/cli@2026.4.0, and the malicious code was published in 'bw1.js,' a file included in the package contents," the

items.title: Microsoft: Some Teams users can’t join meetings after Edge update
items.description: Microsoft confirmed that a recent Microsoft Edge browser update introduced a bug that prevents Windows users from joining Teams meetings. [...]

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: $290M DeFi Hack, macOS LotL Abuse, ProxySmart SIM Farms +25 New Stories
items.description: You scroll past one incident and see another that feels familiar, like it should have been fixed years ago, but it still works with small changes. Same bugs. Same mistakes. The supply chain is messy. Packages you did not check are stealing data, adding backdoors, and spreading. Attacking the systems behind apps is easier than breaking the apps themselves. The exploits are simple but still work

items.title: [Webinar] Mythos Reality Check: Beating Automated Exploitation at AI Speed
items.description: Imagine a world where hackers don't sleep, don't take breaks, and find weak spots in your systems instantly. Well, that world is already here. Thanks to AI, attackers are now launching automated, large-scale exploits faster than ever before. The time you have to fix a vulnerability before it gets attacked is shrinking to zero. We call this the Collapsing Exploit Window, and it means your

items.title: CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
Project Glasswing Proved AI Can Find the Bugs. Who's Going to Fix Them?
items.description: Last week, Anthropic announced Project Glasswing, an AI model so effective at discovering software vulnerabilities that they took the extraordinary step of postponing its public release. Instead, the company has given access to Apple, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and a coalition of others to find and patch bugs before adversaries can. Mythos Preview, the model that led to Project Glasswing, found

items.title: Apple Patches Exploited Notification Flaw, (Thu, Apr 23rd)
items.description: Apple yesterday released iOS/iPadOS 26.4.2 and iOS/iPadOS 18.7.8. This update fixes a single Notification Services vulnerability, CVE-2026-28950:

items.title: 'Zealot' Shows What AI's Capable of in Staged Cloud Attack
items.description: The proof of concept revealed AI-based attacks unfold too fast for human defenders to respond, and that AI evinced more autonomous behavior than expected.

items.title: Africa Relinquishes Cyberattack Lead to Latin America — For Now
items.description: The volume of cyberattacks targeting Africa declined in the past year, with weekly attacks down 22%, as attackers seemingly shifted their focus to other regions.

items.description: Not nearly as polite as the name suggests, the ransomware gang has impressed researchers with its speed in scaling up operations — and its sophistication.

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
